title: In-process batch: `next++` counter is read/incremented between awaits, creating a TOCTOU window
description: In the in-process (test/fake) batch path, `let next = 0` is shared across `conc` coroutines started via `Promise.all`. Each coroutine does `const idx = next++` then immediately awaits `imageIdForPath`. Because JS is single-threaded the increment itself is safe, but the pattern means one coroutine can increment `next` past the valid range before another coroutine's guard `if (idx >= activeFiles.length)` executes. In practice with `conc > 1` all coroutines race to claim indices; no index is claimed twice (JS atomicity), but the identical pattern is also used in `pMapLimit` (line 1054) with the same structural risk. More critically, `nextFile` in the worker-pool path (line 769) is incremented in the same non-atomic pattern inside async dispatcher coroutines — again safe due to JS event loop but fragile if the pattern is copy-pasted to a truly threaded context in the future.

title: Timeout race: the losing `workP` promise is detached and can write files after the function returns
description: When `--timeout-ms` fires, `Promise.race` resolves with the timeout rejection, the `finally` block runs `workP.catch(() => {})` to suppress the unhandled-rejection warning, but `workP` continues running in the background. `ingestImage` returns to the caller (batch), which marks the image as failed and releases the per-image write lock. Meanwhile `workP` may still be executing `applyIngestPlan` — writing level files and renaming the manifest — using the now-released image write lock. This is a real resource-ordering violation: the caller sees 'failed' and may immediately start a retry or GC scan while the timed-out task is still mutating the same image directory.

category: lock ordering / missing lock on abort path
title: Abort handler terminates workers and rejects pending promises without releasing per-image write locks
description: The `onAbort` signal handler (line 817) calls `w.terminate()` on all workers and then rejects all pending promises via `p.reject(...)`. The per-image write locks (`imgLock`) held by dispatcher coroutines are released in each coroutine's `finally` block (line 908), but the `onAbort` handler bypasses that path — it rejects the `await p` promise, which then throws into the `catch` at line 897, which executes `forceFlushCheckpoint` and falls through to `finally { await imgLock?.release() }`. However, there is a gap: if `onAbort` fires between `imgLock = await acquireImageWriteLock(...)` (success) and `const p = new Promise(...)` (i.e., the dispatcher has the lock but hasn't set the pending entry yet), the `dead.has(wi)` guard at line 841 will cause the coroutine to `break` without releasing the lock. The lock file then persists until its 24h stale timeout.

title: Checkpoint state (`cpState`, `inFlight`, `result`) is mutated by multiple concurrent coroutines without serialization
description: Both the in-process path (multiple `run()` coroutines via `Promise.all`) and the worker-pool dispatcher path (multiple `runOne()` dispatchers via `Promise.all`) concurrently mutate `cpState.completed`, `cpState.failed`, `inFlight`, `result.written`, `result.skipped`, and `result.failed`. While JavaScript's event loop prevents true simultaneous mutation, interleaving at `await` boundaries means one coroutine can read a snapshot of `inFlight` as a Set and call `.add(path)` while another coroutine's `persistInFlightImmediate` is in mid-flight writing a stale snapshot. The checkpoint written to disk therefore may transiently omit paths that are genuinely in-flight, reducing its crash-recovery value. `scheduleCompletedPersist` uses a debounce timer but `completedDirty` is also mutated concurrently, so the dirty flag can be cleared by one coroutine while another has pending unflushed completions.

category: TOCTOU / missing lock during GC scan
title: GC orphan scan has a TOCTOU gap between readdir(levelsDir) and the per-file stat/unlink
description: In `removeOrphans`, `levelFiles = await readdir(levelsDir)` captures the directory listing at one point in time. The loop then `await stat(full)` and `await unlink(full)` for each entry serially. Between `readdir` and `unlink`, a concurrent ingest (on a different image) may have written this level file (it was new since the readdir snapshot was taken and thus not in `referenced`). The 10-minute grace window (`GRACE_MS`) mitigates this but does not eliminate it for freshly written files older than 10 minutes that happen to be written by a concurrent shard. The advisory write lock held by `--gc` at the gallery level does block other gallery writes, but `acquireImageWriteLock` for individual images is finer-grained and does not prevent concurrent level writes under a different imageId from racing the GC.

title: WASM singleton init: `initialized` flag set after `await init(...)` but `initPromise` checked before, so concurrent callers all await the same promise safely, but `initialized` is set inside the promise body and could be observed as `true` by a caller that raced past the `if (initialized)` guard before initialization actually completes
description: The `ensureWasm` guard checks `if (initialized) return` first (line 14), then `if (!initPromise)` (line 15). The `initialized = true` assignment is inside the async IIFE assigned to `initPromise` (line 22). If two callers enter concurrently: both see `initialized === false`, both see `initPromise === null`, the first sets `initPromise` and awaits it, the second also assigns `initPromise` (because the first's `initPromise =` and `await` have a microtask gap). In practice the second concurrent caller overwrites `initPromise` with a new IIFE that calls `readFile` + `init` a second time — double-initializing the WASM module. The comment says 'singleton guard prevents double wasm read+init under any interleaving' but this is incorrect for the specific two-caller race at the `if (!initPromise)` assignment.

title: Module-level `cachedSsim` SSIM lazy-init has the same double-call race as raw-backend ensureWasm
description: The `cachedSsim` variable (line 220) is checked with `if (cachedSsim !== undefined) return cachedSsim` at the top of `getCachedSsimFn`. If two `measureConvergenceProfile` calls run concurrently (e.g., from `attachConverged` which runs levels in parallel via `Promise.all`), both can observe `cachedSsim === undefined` and both will `await import('ssim.js')`. The result is two dynamic imports of the same module (Node deduplicates ESM module evaluation, so the module body only runs once, but the import resolution overhead is duplicated). The second to finish will also overwrite `cachedSsim` with the same function, so the result is idempotent but wasteful. This is a low-severity duplicate-work issue, not a correctness bug.

title: `rm` subcommand acquires per-image lock but then acquires gallery write lock inside the already-held image lock, creating a potential lock-order inversion
description: In the `rm` path (line 304), `acquireImageWriteLock` is acquired first, then `removeImage` is called, and afterwards a second `acquireWriteLock` (gallery-level) is acquired for `rebuildIndex` (line 315). Concurrently, the `batch` subcommand acquires the gallery write lock first (line 415) and then acquires per-image write locks inside `ingestBatch`. This is a classic A-then-B vs B-then-A lock ordering inversion. While the advisory lock implementation uses timeouts (30 s default) rather than blocking indefinitely, a deadlock-equivalent timeout storm can occur if both paths are running concurrently: `rm` holds image lock and waits for gallery lock; `batch` holds gallery lock and waits for image lock on the same imageId.

title: `scheduleCompletedPersist` setTimeout callback's async errors are silently swallowed
description: The `scheduleCompletedPersist` function schedules a `setTimeout` with an `async` callback. If `doPersist` throws after `completedDirty = false` is set (unlikely due to the `.catch(() => {})` guard inside `doPersist`, but possible if the catch itself throws), the error is unhandled. More importantly, once `completedDirty` is set to `false` before the await, any failure of the `doPersist` call leaves state inconsistent: `completedDirty` is `false` (saying 'no pending write needed') but the data was never actually persisted. Subsequent calls to `scheduleCompletedPersist` won't re-schedule because `flushTimer` is `null` and `completedDirty` is `false`.

title: `applyIngestPlan` reads the `levels/` directory once and caches it, but concurrent writers may add files between the snapshot and level writes
description: In `applyIngestPlan`, `readdir(levelsDir)` is called once to build `existing` (a Set of filenames). This snapshot is then used in `writeLevelFiles` to skip level files that already exist. If two concurrent ingest operations are writing levels with the same contenthash at the same moment, both may see the file as absent in their respective snapshots and both will attempt `writeFileAtomic`. The EEXIST handler in `writeFileAtomic` (line 115-119) correctly handles this race condition for content-addressed files, so the outcome is correct. However, the `existing` set can also be stale in the other direction: a file listed in `existing` may have been deleted (by a concurrent GC with a very small grace window) between readdir and the skip decision, causing a level to be missing from disk despite the manifest claiming it exists.

title: Runlog append read-modify-write is not protected by the gallery write lock
description: The runlog file (`.pyramid-ingest.runlog.json`) is read, modified (push + slice), and rewritten at lines 466-477. This read-modify-write is not inside the `try` block that holds `heldLock` (the gallery write lock) — it occurs after `heldLock.release()` in the `finally` block at line 482 has conceptually ended the protected section (the `finally` runs before the runlog code at line 453). Wait — actually, the finally runs AFTER the return path. Let me re-read: `ingestBatch` returns at line 431, then the runlog code at line 453 is inside the same `try`, so the gallery lock IS still held. However: concurrent shard runs (multiple CLI processes with `--shard`) each hold their own gallery write lock per process, but a sharded run skips `rebuildIndex` — but the runlog write is NOT skipped for sharded runs. Two shard processes can therefore race to read-modify-write the same runlog file with only the tmp-rename strategy as protection against torn writes.

title: Opportunity: `AbortSignal` is not threaded into `workP` / `ingestImage` so timeout cancellation cannot interrupt decode/encode
description: When `timeout-ms` fires, `Promise.race` rejects and `workP` is detached. The WASM decode/encode operations inside `workP` continue to completion with no way to interrupt them. Threading an `AbortSignal` into `ingestImage` → `decodeMaster` → WASM calls would allow the worker to stop early on timeout, freeing RAM and CPU for the next image. Currently the only abort path is at the batch level (signal from CLI AbortController between images).

title: Opportunity: concurrent `forceFlushCheckpoint` calls from multiple coroutines can issue overlapping writes to the same checkpoint file
description: `forceFlushCheckpoint` is `async` and called with `await` from error handlers in every dispatcher coroutine. If two coroutines fail concurrently, both call `await forceFlushCheckpoint()` — but `forceFlushCheckpoint` does not serialize these calls. Both snapshot `Array.from(inFlight)` at different moments and both call `writeCheckpoint` → `writeFileAtomic` → rename. The rename is atomic so only one writer wins the final file, but the losing write's tmp file is cleaned up (EEXIST path). The real concern is that the checkpoint written is whichever coroutine's `Array.from(inFlight)` snapshot happened to be the last rename winner, which may not be the most current state.

category: lock ordering / write-lock acquired inside read-lock wait
title: Read-lock acquisition re-checks write lock after claiming read lock but does not re-release before timeout check, creating a retry storm
description: In `acquireReadLockFile`, step 3 (line 145) checks if a writer raced between step 1 and step 2. If a writer is present, the read lock is released and the loop continues. But the timeout check on line 148 happens AFTER the unlink, meaning the function may release the read lock file it just created, check the timeout, find it exceeded, and throw — leaving the caller without any lock but also without a clear error about which step failed. Additionally, `readLockPath` is constructed with a unique pid+random suffix (line 164 in cli.ts callers), but the path is created inside `acquireReadLockFile` from the caller-provided argument. Multiple concurrent readers on the same outDir are fine, but the stale-check logic in `waitForReadLocksToClear` reads every read lock file serially without caching, making the drain O(readers) per iteration — a potential slowdown for large concurrent validate fleets.
